message:
📌 *Dqn*
• DQN uses a neural net to estimate future rewards for actions, picking the one with the highest learned value.
• This works by learning from experience, but value function approximation and exploration are still hard; breaks easily.

📌 *Reranking Systems*
• Reranking refines initial retrieval by meticulously scoring a small candidate set for higher precision.
• It's critical when recall is easy but identifying the *best* items from many similar options is hard.

📌 *Mdp*
• MDPs model sequential decision-making; agent picks actions in states to maximize rewards, with outcomes dependent on current state.
• Critical for optimal control in dynamic systems; breaks down if state observations are incomplete (POMDP needed).

📌 *Isolation Forest*
• Outliers are identified by how few random splits it takes to isolate them in a tree structure.
• This method shines with high-dimensional data, but struggles with tightly clustered anomalies or global density variations.

📌 *Cvar*
• CVaR tells you the average hit you take when losses exceed your VaR threshold.
• It's critical for understanding "how bad" things get, especially when tails are fat.

📌 *Yield Curve*
• The yield curve plots a nation's government bond interest rates across maturities, typically rising with longer terms.
• Its shape reflects market expectations for interest rates and economic growth; an inverted curve often signals recession risk.

📌 *Eigenvalues Eigenvectors*
• Eigenvectors point in directions unchanged by a linear transformation; their eigenvalues are the scaling factors.
• Useful for simplifying complex matrix operations, but works only for square matrices that are diagonalizable.

📌 *Rmse Metric*
• RMSE is your average prediction error, but big mistakes hurt disproportionately, measured in the same units as what you're forecasting.
• Use RMSE for regression where large errors are costly; if RMSE is much higher than MAE, you likely have problematic outliers.

📌 *Drawdown*
• Drawdown is your portfolio's percentage drop from its highest prior value.
• Max Drawdown (MDD) quantifies worst-case loss; Calmar ratio uses it to gauge risk-adjusted returns.

📌 *Perplexity Eval*
• Perplexity quantifies a language model's uncertainty – lower means it's less 'surprised' by the text.
• It's an effective branching factor; high perplexity indicates the model is struggling to predict.

📌 *K Nearest Neighbors*
• KNN classifies new data by finding its 'nearest neighbors' in existing data and using their majority vote.
• It's computationally expensive at prediction and sensitive to feature scaling and the choice of K.

📌 *Map Estimation*
• MAP estimation adds a prior belief about model parameters to MLE, helping to regularize the solution.
• It's crucial when data is sparse, but a strong or misinformed prior can bias the model misleadingly.

📌 *Bleu*
• BLEU scores machine translation quality by comparing n-gram overlap with human references, accounting for word order and completeness.
• It's a standard metric but struggles with semantic meaning, synonyms, or evaluating translations for creative text.

📌 *Speech To Text*
• Speech-to-text converts spoken audio to text, typically using deep learning models like Whisper to process audio features.
• Accuracy is measured by Word Error Rate (WER); robust models handle various languages and accents, but struggle with noisy data or specific domain jargon.

📌 *Vision Language Models*
• VLMs combine image and text understanding, converting visual content into "visual tokens" processed by a large language model.
• They excel at cross-modal tasks like visual QA but can struggle with fine-grained visual details or complex spatial reasoning.

📌 *Law Of Large Numbers*
• The more data points you collect, the closer your sample average gets to the actual underlying average.
• Crucial for statistical inference and risk modeling; breaks down if observations aren't independent or identically distributed.

📌 *Llm Eval Frameworks*
• LLM evaluation frameworks automate quality checks for your AI app using other LLMs as judges.
• They're crucial for scaling RAG quality; without them, manual review becomes a crippling bottleneck.

📌 *Causal Lm*
• Causal LMs predict the next word given everything observed so far, left-to-right, using a masked attention mechanism.
• This architecture for GPT-like models excels at text generation, but can't "see" future context even when available.

📌 *Greeks*
• Greeks quantify options sensitivity: Delta for price, Gamma for delta's change, Theta for time decay, Vega for volatility.
• Delta neutrality eliminates stock price risk, but Gamma exposes you to cost of rebalancing as stock moves.

📌 *Neural Architecture Search*
• NAS finds optimal neural network designs by searching architectures, replacing manual trial and error.
• bilevel optimization (DARTS) is key to solving this complex search, but can suffer from architecture collapse.

📌 *Taylor Series*
• Taylor series approximates functions locally with polynomials; effectively, it's a "zoomed-in" polynomial representation.
• It matters for optimization (Newton's method), hedging derivatives, and understanding model sensitivities, but breaks down far from the expansion point.

📌 *Bootstrap*
• Bootstrap replays your experiment many times by drawing samples with replacement to estimate a statistic's distribution.
• It provides robust confidence intervals without strong distributional assumptions, but struggles with extreme tail events or tiny datasets.

📌 *Gru*
• GRU simplifies LSTMs by combining gates and states, using one update gate (z_t) and one reset gate (r_t) for memory.
• This design offers similar performance with ~25% fewer parameters, suitable when computational efficiency is critical.

📌 *Swaps*
• A swap means you bet on fixed vs. floating rates; only interest difference changes hands, no principal.
• Fixed-payer swaps shorten portfolio duration, receive-fixed extends it – critical for hedging.

📌 *Feature Stores*
• A feature store centralizes ML feature definitions and stores their values for consistent access.
• Without one, training data often differs from serving data, causing production model degradation.

📌 *Rouge*
• ROUGE scores quantify how much of the human-written summary your model successfully captured, focusing on recall.
• Useful for evaluating text summarisation; however, it struggles with paraphrasing and capturing semantic similarity beyond exact word matches.

📌 *Correlation Risk*
•  Correlation risk is when asset relationships change, often spiking to 1 when you need diversification most.
•  Historically negative equity-bond correlation flipped with recent inflation; diversification fails in crises.

📌 *Cosine Annealing*
• Cosine annealing schedules learning rates using a cosine curve, smoothly decreasing from high to low.
• This smooth transition avoids abrupt drops of step decay, offering better convergence without jarring the optimizer.

📌 *Mode*
• The mode identifies the most common observation in a dataset.
• It's the only central tendency for categorical data, but can be non-unique (multi-modal) or non-existent in continuous data.

📌 *Cross Validation*
• Train your model multiple times on different data subsets, testing each time on unseen parts.
• This gives a more reliable estimate of real-world performance than a single train/validation split.

📌 *Bert Architecture*
• BERT's full bidirectional attention allows deep contextual understanding by seeing all tokens, unlike models that only look left.
• This bidirectional view excels at tasks like text classification or QA, but makes it fundamentally unsuitable for generating new text autoregressively.

📌 *Real Assets*
• Real assets are tangible things generating income directly, like property or infrastructure, not financial claims.
• They diversify portfolios due to physical supply/demand, but reported valuations often understate true volatility.

📌 *Evaluation Under Imbalance*
• When data is imbalanced, accuracy gives a false sense of security; it's a terrible metric.
• Focus on metrics like PR-AUC or F1-score for the minority class, which truly assess performance.

📌 *Multi Armed Bandit*
• MAB balances trying new options (exploration) with sticking to known good ones (exploitation) to maximize returns.
• It's foundational for balancing quick wins with discovering better long-term strategies, e.g., A/B testing or portfolio optimization.

📌 *Adagrad*
• Adagrad scales learning rates inversely to the square root of past gradients for each parameter.
• This adapts learning to parameter volatility but the decay to a zero learning rate is a major drawback.

📌 *Vectors Matrices*
• Vectors are ordered lists of numbers pointing in a specific direction; matrices are grids that transform these vectors.
• This transformation is fundamental to modeling data movement, like price fluctuations or portfolio rebalancing.

📌 *Mean*
• The mean is the center of gravity of your data; it's where everything balances out.
• It minimizes squared errors, fundamental to regression; but outliers can drastically pull it away from the typical value.

📌 *Benchmark Selection*
• Choosing the right benchmark defines what "good performance" means for your portfolio.
• A misaligned benchmark distorts risk assessment and incentivizes the wrong investment decisions.

📌 *Tool Calling Patterns*
• Tool calling patterns are common ways AI agents orchestrate external functions: sequential, parallel, or conditional execution, similar to software design patterns.
• Parallel calls boost efficiency by executing multiple tools concurrently; sequential ensures logical data flow, but can be a bottleneck.

📌 *Lof*
• LOF flags data points that are significantly more isolated than their immediate surroundings, essentially "lonely" among their peers.
• It's powerful for detecting contextual anomalies, but can be noisy with extremely high-dimensional or sparse data.

📌 *Concept Drift*
• Concept drift is your model's target changing how it maps inputs to outputs. Fraudsters changing tactics is a classic example.
• Ignoring it means your deployed models degrade severely over time, leading to poor predictions and significant business impact.

📌 *Huber Loss*
• Huber loss neatly blends MSE's smoothness for small errors with MAE's outlier robustness for large errors, via a tunable delta.
• This ensures stable gradients during optimization while limiting the impact of extreme outliers on model updates.

📌 *Bayesian Optimization Hpo*
• Bayesian optimization intelligently samples hyperparameters by modeling performance and uncertainty, guiding searches efficiently.
• It excels with expensive evaluations but struggles with high-dimensional spaces or non-stationary objectives.

📌 *Etl Vs Elt*
• ETL transforms data then loads it; ELT loads raw data first, then transforms within the data warehouse.
• ETL suits legacy systems or PII masking; ELT leverages cloud warehouse power for flexible, scalable transformations.

📌 *Lasso Regression*
• Lasso penalizes the sum of absolute coefficients, driving some to exactly zero for automatic feature selection.
• This sparsity is powerful for high-dimensional data, but can be unstable if features are highly correlated.

📌 *Agent Loop*
• An AI agent repeatedly observes, thinks using an LLM, and acts with tools to achieve a goal.
• This loop enables complex, multi-step tasks but can get stuck if tools fail or thoughts mislead.

📌 *Mae Loss*
• MAE measures average prediction error as simple distance, ignoring direction; easy to interpret because it's in the same units as your data.
• Robust to outliers and minimizes the conditional median, making it suitable when extreme predictions are particularly undesirable.

📌 *Logistic Regression*
• Predicts probability from 0 to 1 (e.g., spam/not spam) using a sigmoid function applied to a linear combination of features.
• Training maximizes log-likelihood instead of minimizing squared error; iterative optimization is required as no closed-form solution exists.

📌 *Chain Of Thought*
• Chain-of-Thought (CoT) makes large language models (LLMs) break down complex problems into manageable steps before answering.
• This simple technique significantly boosts LLM accuracy on multi-step reasoning, turning tough problems into solvable series of simpler thoughts.

📌 *Gradient Clipping*
• Gradient clipping shrinks overly large gradients to a predefined maximum, preventing extreme weight updates that destabilize training.
• Without it, exploding gradients can drastically increase loss, making your model diverge instead of converging effectively.

📌 *Class Weights*
• Class weights penalize errors on underrepresented data more, balancing the loss contribution of all classes.
• Use them to improve model performance on rare events; ignore them and your model won't care about the tricky cases.

📌 *Lambda Architecture*
• Combines batch (accurate, historical) and speed (real-time, recent) processing for comprehensive data views.
• Essential when both complete data accuracy and immediate results are critical, but maintaining two divergent systems adds complexity.

📌 *Tf Idf*
• TF-IDF identifies words unique and important to a specific document by balancing within-document frequency with rarity across the entire dataset.
• Crucial for information retrieval; however, BM25 improves ranking by considering term frequency limits and document length.

📌 *F1 Score*
• F1 score tells you how well your model balances finding all positives and avoiding false alarms.
• Use F1 when both false positives and false negatives are costly, and a balance is crucial.

📌 *Data Warehouses*
• Data warehouses are separate, read-optimized databases built for complex analytical questions, not live transactions.
• Their columnar storage and denormalized schemas enable fast queries on massive datasets, crucial for business intelligence.

📌 *Bonds*
•  Bonds are simply lending money: you get regular interest payments and your principal back later.
•  Their price moves inversely to interest rates, impacting portfolio value and ideal entry/exit points.

📌 *Confusion Matrix*
• A confusion matrix breaks down classification correctness into four outcomes (true/false positive/negative).
• It's crucial for understanding model errors beyond simple accuracy, especially with imbalanced data, by assessing false alarms (precision) and missed detections (recall).

📌 *Perceptron*
• Perceptron classifies by drawing a line; it outputs 1 if weighted inputs exceed a threshold, -1 otherwise.
• It only learns from its errors; guarantees classification of perfectly separable data in finite steps.

📌 *Information Ratio*
• Information Ratio (IR) quantifies how much extra return a portfolio manager achieves for each unit of risk taken relative to a benchmark.
• A high IR signifies a skilled manager effectively adding value; a low IR indicates active risk without commensurate reward.

📌 *Automl*
• AutoML streamlines model

📌 *Sample Space Events*
• Define the sample space first; it's the foundation for any probabilistic analysis.
• Mis-specifying the sample space invalidates all subsequent probability calculations.

📌 *Activation Functions*
• Activation functions give neural nets their power, letting them learn complex, non-linear relationships beyond simple lines.
• While ReLU is efficient, be wary of "dead neurons" if inputs consistently fall below zero.

📌 *P Value*
• Low p-value means data is unlikely *if* your null hypothesis is true; this is evidence *against* the null.
• P-values don't tell you the probability of your hypothesis being true, just how surprising your data is under specific assumptions.

📌 *Z Test*
• A Z-test tells you if your sample mean is statistically different from a hypothesized population mean.
• Use it when you know the population standard deviation or have a large sample; otherwise, results are unreliable.

📌 *Mean Variance Optimization*
• MVO finds the portfolio weights that yield the highest return for a given risk or lowest risk for a given return.
• It breaks down with estimated parameters; errors in covariance often lead to concentrated, poorly diversified portfolios.

📌 *Gradient Descent Math*
• Gradient descent updates a model by repeatedly nudging it opposite to the steepest ascent of the error function.
• This simple iterative move works because the negative gradient always points to a local minimum, unless you overshoot.

📌 *Central Limit Theorem*
• Sample means, regardless of underlying distribution, converge to a Normal distribution with sufficient sample size.
• Crucial for statistical inference in finance; breaks with dependent data or infinite variance.

📌 *Message Passing*
• Nodes exchange information with neighbors, then update themselves based on collected "messages."
• This common pattern underpins most GNNs; aggregation choice impacts expressiveness for graph tasks.

📌 *Positional Encoding*
• Transformers lack inherent word order; positional encoding injects this crucial sequence information into embeddings.
• This allows parallel processing while preserving meaning, but complex dependencies across very long sequences can still be challenging.

📌 *Dqn*
• DQN learns optimal moves by predicting future rewards for each action using a neural network.
• Experience replay helps stabilize training by decorrelating consecutive observations, avoiding catastrophic forgetting.

📌 *Random Search*
• Random search picks hyperparameter values randomly within defined ranges, unlike grid search which tries every combination.
• It's surprisingly efficient when only a few hyperparameters significantly impact performance, outperforming exhaustive grid searches often.

📌 *Causal Inference*
• Causal inference tries to measure how much X changes Y, not just if they move together.
• It's critical for decision-making; mistaking correlation for causation leads to bad interventions.

📌 *Randomized Control Trials*
• RCTs randomly split people into experiment vs. control groups to isolate treatment effect.
• This ensures observed outcome differences are from the treatment, not hidden factors.

📌 *Variance*
• Variance quantifies data dispersion; larger deviations from the mean are disproportionately weighted.
• High variance suggests noisy data or a wide range of outcomes, making predictions less reliable.

📌 *Mse Loss*
• MSE penalizes large errors disproportionately, as it squares the difference between predicted and actual values.
• This makes it sensitive to outliers, potentially pulling the model towards fitting extreme data points.

📌 *Perplexity*
• Perplexity quantifies a language model's "surprise" by unseen text; lower values mean the model predicts better.
• It's a key metric for LM quality, but doesn't guarantee factual accuracy or useful output.

📌 *Data Drift*
• Your model trained on past data will struggle when the real-world data starts looking significantly different.
• This means your model's predictions become less reliable, potentially leading to incorrect business decisions.

📌 *Online Learning*
• Online learning refines models incrementally with new data, ideal for dynamic environments.
• Essential for real-time systems; watch out for concept drift or instability with poor update strategies.

📌 *Hybrid Search*
• Hybrid search merges keyword (BM25) and semantic (dense) relevance for a more complete understanding of queries.
• This blend improves retrieval quality for varied query types; RRF effectively combines results without score normalization.

📌 *Hierarchical Clustering*
• Hierarchical clustering groups similar things iteratively, like building a family tree for your data points.
• Use Ward linkage for compact clusters, cutting the dendrogram at different heights gives you varying granularities of grouping.

📌 *Bagging*
• Train multiple models on re-sampled data, then average their outputs to get a more robust prediction.
• This dampens noise (variance), making models more stable, especially for unstable algorithms like decision trees.

📌 *Prompt Injection Defense*
• Prompt injection makes AI do bad things by sneaking commands into its input, forcing it to betray its original purpose.
• It's a fundamental context-window vulnerability; indirect injection is especially nasty as the attacker's power extends beyond the direct prompt.

📌 *Decision Tree Regressor*
• Decision trees predict numbers by repeatedly splitting data based on feature questions, like narrowing down options to a specific average.
• Splits are chosen locally to reduce error, meaning the overall optimal solution isn't guaranteed; local greed doesn't always find global best.

📌 *Rnn*
• RNNs process

📌 *Rag*
• RAG retrieves relevant information from a knowledge base to augment an LLM's response, grounding it in specific facts.
• This minimizes hallucination for specific factual queries and enables dynamic knowledge updates without constant model retraining.

📌 *Tecton*
• Tecton takes the pain out of feature engineering and serving, offering a fully managed platform based on the Feast experience.
• It's your go-to for production-ready ML features, but remember, vendor lock-in is a consideration.

📌 *Llama*
• Llama showed smaller models, trained on vastly more data, could beat much larger, less-trained competitors.
• Its architectural choices became the blueprint for efficient, high-performing open-source LLMs crucial for many applications.

📌 *Model Registry*
• Model registry is where all your trained models live, with their versions and details.
• Without it, managing, deploying, and reproducing models becomes a chaotic nightmare.

📌 *Tracking Error*
• Tracking error quantifies how much a portfolio's returns veer from its benchmark, expressed as the standard deviation of their return differences.
• It's critical for active management evaluation and risk budgeting; predicting it is harder than observing it, often revealing factor bets.

📌 *Survivorship Bias*
• Survivorship bias is judging a strategy only by its winners, overlooking failures that didn't make the cut.
• In finance, this inflates backtest returns by ignoring bankrupt or delisted companies, misrepresenting true performance.

📌 *Pre Training*
• Pre-training is a model's foundational education, where it learns broad patterns by predicting the next word across massive datasets.
• This phase establishes its core knowledge and capabilities; its scale dictates downstream performance and compute efficiency.

📌 *One Hot Encoding*
• Convert text categories like 'red' or 'M' into numbers for ML models to understand the data.
• For linear models, drop one category to avoid perfect collinearity; tree models handle all categories.

📌 *Nosql*
• NoSQL databases ditch fixed tables for flexible data models, perfect for handling diverse, unstructured data at scale.
• They trade strict consistency (like SQL) for availability and fault tolerance, a pragmatic choice for modern, distributed systems.

📌 *Logistic Regression Stats*
• Logistic regression transforms binary outcomes into a linear combination of predictors using log-odds.
• Coefficients represent multiplicative changes in the odds; interpret them carefully given the non-linear link function.

📌 *Interaction Features*
* Interaction features reveal how variables modify each other's impact, capturing insights linear models miss.
* Crucial for conditional effects but explosive in dimensionality; prune inputs before generating.

📌 *Convex Optimization Portfolio*
• Portfolio optimization under standard assumptions (mean-variance) is a Quadratic Program (QP), efficiently solved for a global optimum.
• Convexity guarantees reliable solutions; non-convexities like market impact or transaction costs require careful modeling to preserve solvable problem structure.

📌 *Instruction Tuning*
• Train models on diverse, instruction-formatted tasks to enable zero-shot generalization across new, unseen tasks.
• Using many instruction templates per task prevents brittle template memorization and improves robustness.

📌 *Kl Divergence*
• KL divergence quantifies the information lost when approximating one probability distribution with another.
• It's asymmetric and penalizes more heavily when the approximating distribution assigns zero probability to events that actually occur.

📌 *Rest Api Serving*
•  Serving ML models via REST API means standard HTTP requests (e.g., POST with input, JSON response with predictions) deliver your model's intelligence.
•  This universal approach simplifies integration, but performance bottlenecks and robust error handling become critical at scale.

📌 *Mode*
• The mode identifies the most common observation in your data set.
• It's primarily useful for categorical data, as it loses practical meaning with truly continuous data.

📌 *Mcp*
• MCP standardizes AI integration with external services, allowing AI models to leverage tools, resources, and templates efficiently.
• It streamlines development by abstracting tool connectivity, but its effectiveness depends on widespread adoption and robust tool server implementations.

📌 *Ddpg*
• DDPG learns a Q-function *and* a deterministic policy, allowing direct action output for continuous control.
• It's sample-efficient due to off-policy learning and replay buffers, but can be brittle with hyperparameter tuning.

📌 *Universal Approximation Theorem*
• Any continuous function can be approximated by a single-hidden-layer neural network with enough neurons.
• This ensures expressive power, but doesn't guarantee efficient learning or generalization beyond observed data.

📌 *Yield Curve*
• Yield curve maps bond yields against maturity; typically, longer loans mean higher returns for investors.
• Most yield curve changes are driven by overall rate shifts, steepness, or the curve's bend.

📌 *Distributed Training*
• Distributed training means different machines handle parts of the work, speeding up complex model training significantly.
• It's critical for large models or datasets, but synchronization overhead can bottleneck performance if not designed properly.

📌 *Reranking*
• Re-ranking refines initial search results: a fast first pass gets candidates, then a slower, smarter model picks the best ones.
• It's crucial for precision where initial recall is broad; cross-encoders excel here but beware of their O(k) complexity for large k.

📌 *Quality Factor*
• Quality factor targets fundamentally sound companies: profitable, stable, low debt.
• Combines well with value, avoiding "value traps" by selecting financially healthy cheap stocks.

📌 *Cross Entropy*
• Cross-entropy quantifies how well your predicted distribution matches the true distribution, weighted by the true probabilities.
• Minimizing it forces your model's outputs (q) to align with the actual labels (p), critical for classification tasks.

📌 *Llamaindex*
• LlamaIndex connects LLMs to your data, primarily focused on loading, indexing, and retrieving information for RAG.
• Use it when you need sophisticated data ingestion and retrieval strategies beyond simple top-k, especially for complex documents.

📌 *Rouge*
• ROUGE scores how much of the human-written summary your model's summary managed to include. It's a recall metric.
• Essential for evaluating summarization, but raw overlap can miss semantic nuance and fluent rephrasing.

📌 *Lasso Regression*
• Lasso uses an L1 penalty to shrink some coefficients to exactly zero, effectively performing automatic feature selection.
• This means Lasso is excellent for high-dimensional data where you suspect many features are irrelevant to the target.

📌 *Pca*
• PCA transforms data to new axes that capture maximum variance, ordered by importance.
• Useful for dimensionality reduction, but highly sensitive to data scaling and works best with linear relationships.

📌 *Convolution Operation*
• Convolution sweeps a small pattern (kernel) across data (image, time series), calculating local summaries.

• This extracts relevant features efficiently, but loses global context and can struggle with rotations/scale.

📌 *Few Shot Prompting*
• Few-shot prompting guides LLMs by providing a few input-output examples directly in the prompt, demonstrating desired response style.
• It's efficient for quick adaptation without training, but struggles if demonstrations are misleading or context contradicts implicit knowledge.

📌 *Matrix Factorization*
• Matrix factorization breaks down complex interactions (like users rating items) into underlying features, then rebuilds predictions from these features.
• It's powerful for recommendations but struggles with cold-start users/items due to lack of observed data.

📌 *Edge Deployment*
• Edge deployment places ML models directly on devices, enabling real-time, local inference.
• Critical for low-latency applications and privacy; breaks when model size or compute demands exceed device capability.

📌 *Correlation Risk*
• Your investment diversification hinges on assets behaving differently, but this relationship isn't static.
• During market stress, these beneficial correlations often break, leading to unexpected portfolio losses.

📌 *Visualization Techniques*
• Visualizing data quickly reveals patterns, distributions, and outliers that raw numbers obscure.
• Effective visuals prioritize position and length for accurate comparison; avoid pie charts for this.

📌 *Bayesian Vs Frequentist*
• Frequentists see parameters as fixed unknowns; Bayesians treat them as uncertain variables, updating beliefs with data.

• This distinction shapes how we interpret confidence intervals and reason about uncertainty, especially with limited data.

📌 *Momentum Factor*
• Assets that have performed well tend to keep performing well; assets that have done poorly, poorly.
• This strong effect crashes hard when markets snap back; vol-scaling helps manage that risk.

📌 *Stratified Sampling*
• Break your data into meaningful, distinct groups, then randomly sample from each.

• Reduces sampling error by capturing population diversity; fails if strata aren't well-defined or overlap.

📌 *Layer Norm*
• LayerNorm standardises activations across features for each individual input, independent of other samples.
• It's robust to varying batch sizes and crucial for sequence models since it doesn't rely on batch statistics.

📌 *Kurtosis*
*   Kurtosis tells you how "spiky" your data is and how likely extreme values are compared to a bell curve.
*   High kurtosis means more big wins/losses than expected, critical for risk and financial modeling, where infrequent extremes hit hard.

📌 *Feature Stores*
• A feature store centralizes ML feature data, acting as a single source of truth for both training and serving.
• Without point-in-time correctness, you'll leak future data into training, leading to models that fail in production.

📌 *Credit Spread*
• Credit spread is the added yield you demand for corporate debt over risk-free government bonds due to default risk.
• It quantifies perceived risk; widening spreads signal increasing investor concern about credit quality and economic outlook.

📌 *Capm*
• Your investment's expected return is all about its market beta: how much it dances with the overall market.
• If beta is your only risk measure, CAPM matters. Otherwise, it's just one piece of the puzzle.

📌 *Backpropagation*
• Backpropagation quantifies each weight's error contribution by propagating output error signals backward through the network using calculus.
• It's costly: requires storing all activations during forward pass and doubles computation for gradients, impacting large models.

📌 *Stationarity*
• A stationary series has consistent statistical properties (mean, variance, lag correlations) over time.
• Non-stationarity invalidates standard time series models; random walks are a classic example.

📌 *Reranking Systems*
• Reranking refines initial broad search results, using a second, more powerful model to pick the best few from a larger set.
• It drastically improves result quality by sacrificing speed on a small, already candidate-filtered set.

📌 *Stemming Lemmatization*
• Stemming chops off word endings quickly, often producing partial words, while lemmatization uses dictionary lookup to find the true base form.
• Use lemmatization when precision and valid words are critical for downstream tasks like sentiment analysis; stemming works for quick indexing where speed trumps accuracy.

📌 *Cross Entropy Loss*
• Measures how much your model underestimated the probability of the actual outcome.
• Excellent for classification: provides a clear, well-behaved gradient for optimization.

📌 *Rmsprop*
• RMSprop stabilizes AdaGrad by forgetting old gradients, using an exponential average of squared gradients instead of a sum.
• This prevents learning rates from shrinking too aggressively, enabling continued learning in deep networks.

📌 *Mcmc*
• MCMC lets you simulate complex probability distributions by moving randomly but strategically.
• It's crucial for Bayesian inference when direct calculation of the posterior is intractable.

📌 *Dqn*
• DQN learns to estimate future rewards for actions using a neural network, then picks the action with the highest estimated reward.
• It achieved superhuman performance in Atari by leveraging deep learning and experience replay to stabilize Q-learning.

📌 *Bertscore*
• BERTScore uses deep contextual embeddings to gauge semantic similarity between texts, going beyond exact word matches.
• It's robust to synonyms and paraphrases unlike older metrics, but can struggle with subtle meaning changes contextually.

📌 *Json Mode*
• JSON mode ensures your LLM output is always valid JSON, no matter its internal structure or fields.
• Useful for basic JSON parsing where schema isn't enforced; breaks if you need specific data structures.

📌 *Llm As Judge*
• LLM-as-Judge uses a strong AI to rate other AI outputs, replacing human judgment for speed and cost.
• GPT-4 as judge agrees 81% with humans; pairwise comparisons are key, not absolute scoring.

📌 *Confounding Variables*
• Confounders are hidden influences: they make X and Y look related when a third factor, Z, is actually driving both.
• Ignoring confounders gives you misleading results; adjusting for them reveals the true relationships.

📌 *Tool Calling Patterns*
• Tool calling patterns generalize how AI agents use external functions: sequentially for dependent steps, or in parallel for independent actions.
• These patterns dictate efficiency and control flow; misuse leads to stalled agents or unnecessary latency.

📌 *Emergent Abilities*
• Large models spontaneously develop capabilities not seen in smaller versions, unlocking new problem-solving approaches.
• These abilities often require certain scale thresholds, and trying them on smaller models can actually degrade performance.

📌 *Data Lakes*
• A data lake is raw, cheap storage for everything, enabling "schema-on-read" with formats like JSON, CSV, and Parquet.
• Delta Lake adds ACID transactions and time travel to Parquet files, making data lakes reliable for ML and finance operations.

📌 *Bivariate Analysis*
• Bivariate analysis cuts through noise to see how pairs of variables interact.
• Essential for feature selection and understanding dependencies; univariate or multivariate perspectives can miss critical pairwise relationships.

📌 *Message Passing*
• Nodes exchange information with neighbors, then update their state based on combined messages (M, A, U functions).
• Enables diverse GNN architectures; aggregation choice (e.g., sum) impacts model expressiveness.

📌 *Nlp Finance*
• NLP sifts through financial text to find hidden cues about market movements or company health, beyond just the numbers.
• Lexicons like LM provide quick sentiment signals; deeper models like FinBERT offer precise context for complex financial data interpretation.

📌 *Correlation*
• Correlation quantifies how two things move: together (positive), opposite (negative), or completely independently (zero).
• Pearson works for linear relationships; use Spearman or Kendall for non-linear, monotonic patterns or skewed data.

📌 *Model Batching*
• Batching aggregates multiple prediction requests, letting GPUs process many inputs in parallel for efficiency.
• It drastically cuts inference latency for high-throughput systems, but small batches can starve the GPU.

📌 *Fundamental Analysis*
• Fundamental analysis gauges a company's true value, beyond market noise, by dissecting its business strength.
• Strong businesses earn more than their cost of capital (ROIC > WACC), creating lasting value; others destroy it.

📌 *Content Moderation*
• AI content moderation uses real-time safety gates plus async checks on generated content samples.
• It combines ML with tiered human review to catch severe to borderline policy violations.

📌 *Gradient Boosting*
• Gradient Boosting sequentially builds weak models, each correcting the cumulative errors of its predecessors.
• It excels on complex, non-linear data but can overfit if not carefully tuned, especially with noisy financial time series.

📌 *Svr*
• SVR optimizes for a tube around the data, penalizing deviations only if they fall outside this "epsilon" margin.
• This ε-insensitivity prioritizes major errors, but selecting the right ε is crucial to avoid underfitting or overfitting.

📌 *Gans*
• A GAN is a competitive game between a generator creating fakes and a discriminator identifying them, both learning to improve.
• This

📌 *Rmse Metric*
• RMSE quant

📌 *Parameter Servers*
• Parameter servers centralize model parameters for distributed training; workers fetch parameters, compute gradients, and push updates.
• Ideal for very large, sparse models (e.g., recommenders) and resilient asynchronous updates, but can bottleneck with dense models.

📌 *Concept Drift*
• Concept drift means model inputs now predict outputs differently; previous patterns are stale.
• It breaks models expecting stable relationships, requiring retraining or adaptive algorithms.

📌 *Agent Loop*
• An agent repeatedly observes its environment, thinks based on that observation (often with an LLM), then acts, cycling until it completes a task.
• This loop is fundamental to autonomous AI, but failures often stem from poor observation, flawed thinking, or ineffective action execution.

📌 *Gpt 4V*
• GPT-4V combines text and image inputs to generate text outputs, describing and reasoning about visual data efficiently.
• It excels at multimodal understanding for specialized domains, but performance can degrade with low-detail image tiling.
